ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term exists for Jal Jeera; mapped to broader beverage food product class as the closest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No specific FoodEx2 code for Jal Jeera; A0FJP (water-based beverages) is the closest defensible broader category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No applicable SNOMED CT food concept identified for this traditional Indian spiced beverage.

allergens: gluten (if boondi is added, contains gram flour)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Jal Jeera is a 250 ml glass, providing approximately 60 kcal, 14 g carbohydrates, and 350 mg sodium.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: National Institute of Nutrition (NIN), ICMR, India
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Jal Jeera is a composite spiced beverage. Nutrient values are adapted from NIN India data for individual ingredients (cumin, mint, lemon juice, black salt, water) and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cumin (FDC ID 170918), mint (FDC ID 173474), and lemon juice (FDC ID 167747). The composite per-100g profile reflects a standard preparation diluted in water.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — peer-reviewed analogous estimate for spiced water-based beverage
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Jal Jeera in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 15 is estimated by analogy with dilute spiced water-based beverages containing minimal sugar and no starch. The carbohydrate content is predominantly from trace sugars in lemon juice and amchur; the spice matrix (cumin, ginger) does not contribute glycaemic load. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (15 × 14 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 2.1, classified as very low.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from black salt (kala namak) and added salt; 140 mg sodium per 100 g equates to approximately 350 mg per standard 250 ml serving.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals managing hypertension should limit or avoid Jal Jeera in its standard preparation due to significant sodium from black salt; a low-sodium version using minimal salt and no kala namak may be tolerated in small quantities.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (estimated GI 15) and low carbohydrate content (5.6 g per 100 g); no added refined sugar in traditional preparation.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Jal Jeera is generally suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when prepared without added sugar, as its low GI and minimal carbohydrate load produce negligible postprandial glucose elevation; patients should confirm no sugar is added during preparation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat (0.04 g per 100 g) and zero cholesterol are favorable; however, elevated sodium (140 mg per 100 g) is a concern for cardiovascular risk management.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The lipid profile of Jal Jeera is cardiovascular-friendly due to negligible fat and cholesterol, but the sodium content from black salt warrants caution for patients with heart failure or sodium-restricted cardiovascular conditions; a reduced-salt preparation is advisable.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Cumin and ginger contain bioactive compounds with documented anti-inflammatory properties.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Jal Jeera may be mildly beneficial for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to the anti-inflammatory properties of cumin and ginger; however, very cold preparations should be avoided if cold beverages exacerbate joint symptoms.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Jal Jeera is a traditional North Indian spiced beverage whose name literally translates to 'cumin water' in Hindi. It is prepared by dissolving a concentrated blend of roasted cumin powder, black salt (kala namak), dried mango powder (amchur), fresh ginger, and mint leaves in chilled water, typically finished with fresh lemon juice and occasionally tamarind for added sourness. The resulting drink is simultaneously salty, sour, tangy, and mildly spicy, with a distinctive sulfurous note from the black salt. It is served chilled in tall glasses and is commonly garnished with crunchy boondi (tiny fried gram flour pearls) and fresh mint leaves. Nutritionally, Jal Jeera is a very low-calorie beverage at approximately 24 kcal per 100 g, with minimal protein (0.4 g), fat (0.2 g), and carbohydrates (5.6 g). Its sodium content is relatively high at 140 mg per 100 g due to black salt, making it unsuitable for strict low-sodium diets. It has a very low estimated glycemic index of approximately 15, making it appropriate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when prepared without added sugar. Cumin and ginger contribute trace amounts of iron, vitamin C, and anti-inflammatory phytochemicals. Jal Jeera is widely consumed as a digestive aid, a summer cooler, and an appetizer before meals across North and Central India, and is a staple of Indian street food culture and festive gatherings.
common_user_queries: Is Jal Jeera good for digestion?
How many calories in Jal Jeera?
Is Jal Jeera suitable for diabetics?
Can I drink Jal Jeera if I have high blood pressure?
What spices are in Jal Jeera?
What is the glycemic index of Jal Jeera?
Is Jal Jeera the same as cumin water?

confidence_reason: Jal Jeera is a composite traditional beverage with no single exact entry in any approved primary source. Nutrient values are adapted from NIN India ingredient-level data and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cumin, mint, and lemon juice. The GI is an analogous estimate. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for this traditional spiced water-based beverage are not available in NIN India or USDA FoodData Central as of 2024. water_g is set to -1: moisture content of the final diluted beverage is not reported in the adapted source. 2. cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero: Jal Jeera contains no animal-derived ingredients in its standard preparation; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central ingredient-level data. vitamin_b12_mcg is confirmed zero: the beverage contains no animal-derived ingredients. vitamin_d_mcg is confirmed zero: no animal-derived or fortified ingredients are present. trans_fat_g is confirmed zero per ingredient composition. 3. Sodium value of 140 mg per 100 g reflects standard preparation with black salt; sodium will vary significantly depending on the amount of kala namak and regular salt used. 4. The food_group has been reclassified from 'Snacks' (input) to 'Beverages' as Jal Jeera is a liquid drink. 5. Portions in the input contained five entries with duplicates; consolidated to one canonical serving of 250 ml (250 g). 6. GI glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (15) × available carbohydrate per serving (14 g) / 100 = 2.1 g, classified as very low.
